name: La Almenara and Ermita de Navahonda Loop
name_original: Cerro de La Almenara – circular desde el Observatorio Espacial Robledo de Chavela
description: Hike the 5.6-mile Cerro de La Almenara circular trail, offering moderate difficulty, panoramic views, and a glimpse of the Robledo de Chavel
region_intro: Hiking around Robledo De Chavela offers diverse landscapes, including extensive pine forests, dehesas, and oak groves. The region is situated between the Sierra de Guadarrama and the Sierra de Gredos, providing varied terrain and views. Its municipal territory is largely protected, forming part of the Natura 2000 ecological network, and the Cofio River runs through the area. These features contribute to a rich biodiversity, making it a notable destination for outdoor activities. Best hiking trails around Robledo De Chavela The most popular hiking route is La Almenara and Ermita de Navahonda Loop, a 5.6 miles (9.0 km) trail that takes 3 hours 27 minutes to complete, offering panoramic views. Another top favourite among local hikers is La Almenara – Navahonda Hermitage loop from Robledo de Chavela, a difficult 10.9 miles (17.6 km) path that connects to the Ermita de Navahonda. Local hikers also love the View of Puente Mocha loop from Valdemaqueda, a 4.8 miles (7.8 km) trail leading through varied terrain with views of the Cofio River, often completed in about 2 hours 15 minutes. Hiking around Robledo De Chavela is defined by pine forests, dehesas, and the Cofio River, with views towards the Sierra de Gredos. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy strolls to more challenging treks. The routes in Robledo De Chavela are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from nearly 900 reviews. More than 4,700 hikers have used komoot to explore Robledo De Chavela's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Robledo De Chavela?
A: There are over 240 hiking routes around Robledo De Chavela, offering a wide variety of options for different skill levels and preferences. These include 47 easy routes, 144 moderate routes, and 49 difficult routes.

Q: Are there easy hiking options suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Robledo De Chavela offers numerous easy hiking options. For instance, the region research mentions the 8 km "Ruta Pinar de Robledo de Chavela" as perfect for families to enjoy the pine forest. Overall, there are 47 easy routes available.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes in the area?
A: Yes, circular routes are available. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e957816635/la-almenara-and-ermita-de-navahonda-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Almenara and Ermita de Navahonda Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 9.0 km trail offering panoramic views. The region also features the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14882955" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Puente Mocha loop from Valdemaqueda&lt;/a&gt;, which is 7.8 km and leads through varied terrain with views of the Cofio River.

Q: What kind of landscapes and natural features can I expect to see while hiking?
A: Robledo De Chavela is known for its diverse landscapes, including extensive pine forests, dehesas (wooded pastures), and oak groves. You'll find yourself in a region bridging the Sierra de Guadarrama and the Sierra de Gredos, offering spectacular views. The Cofio River runs through the area, and during the rainy season, seasonal wetlands become a sanctuary for amphibians and migratory birds. Much of the municipal territory is protected within the Natura 2000 ecological network.

Q: Are there any trails with great viewpoints?
A: Absolutely. Many trails offer stunning vistas. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e957816635/la-almenara-and-ermita-de-navahonda-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Almenara and Ermita de Navahonda Loop&lt;/a&gt; is noted for its panoramic views. The Cerro de La Almenara area itself provides excellent panoramic views, and the viewpoint of the Robledillo pine forest offers views towards the Sierra de Gredos. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5317543" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Entrecabezas Spring – Seat of Philip II loop from Zarzalejo&lt;/a&gt; leads to the Chair of Philip II, a notable granite viewpoint with views of the Monastery of El Escorial.

Q: What are some notable attractions or landmarks near the hiking routes?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, you can explore several points of interest. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5149766" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Almenara&lt;/a&gt; summit offers great views. The region is also home to the NASA Space Tracking Station, the Gothic Church of Nuestra Señora de la Asunción (dating to the 15th century), and the Ermita de Navahonda. You might also encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5662891" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Picadas Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2885390" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Juan Reservoir Marina&lt;/a&gt; along some routes.

Q: Is Robledo De Chavela suitable for more challenging hikes?
A: Yes, for experienced hikers seeking a challenge, there are 49 difficult routes available. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/6927597" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Almenara – Navahonda Hermitage loop from Robledo de Chavela&lt;/a&gt;, a challenging 17.6 km path that connects to the Ermita de Navahonda.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Robledo De Chavela?
A: The trails in Robledo De Chavela are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from nearly 900 reviews. Hikers often praise the varied terrain, the beautiful pine forests, and the stunning views towards the Sierra de Gredos.

Q: Are the trails in Robledo De Chavela dog-friendly?
A: While specific regulations for dogs on every trail vary, generally, dogs are welcome on many hiking trails in the Sierra Oeste region, especially if kept on a leash. Given the area's rich biodiversity and protected species like the Iberian Imperial Eagle and Black Stork, it's always recommended to keep dogs under control to protect wildlife.

Q: How can I get to the hiking trails using public transport?
A: Robledo de Chavela is accessible by public transport from Madrid. You can typically take a bus from the capital to the town. Once in Robledo de Chavela, many trailheads are within walking distance or a short local bus ride from the town center. It's advisable to check local bus schedules for specific routes and times.

Q: Is there parking available near the trailheads?
A: Yes, parking is generally available in and around Robledo de Chavela, particularly near popular trail starting points. Many routes begin close to the town or in designated areas that offer parking facilities. It's recommended to arrive early during peak seasons to secure a spot.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Robledo De Chavela?
A: Robledo De Chavela offers hiking opportunities year-round. Spring and autumn are particularly pleasant due to milder temperatures and vibrant natural colors. Summer can be hot, so early morning or late afternoon hikes are preferable. Winter hiking is also possible, with the landscape taking on a different charm, though some higher elevation areas might be colder.

Q: Are there any water features like rivers or reservoirs along the trails?
A: Yes, the Cofio River runs through the area, and several trails offer views or access to it. Additionally, the region is home to reservoirs such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5662891" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Picadas Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2885390" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Juan Reservoir Marina&lt;/a&gt;, providing scenic water features along some routes.
tips: The existence of a disappeared medieval town called Navahonda has led researchers to think that this hermitage was the Church of said town.
Singular Tree of the Community of Madrid No. 308. It is actually a group of singular pines that inhabit this area of the mountains.

The top of La Almenara at 1259 meters above sea level is a unique panoramic point in the region of the Guadarrama River. From here you will have beautiful views of the entire Rio Coño valley and to the other side the Robledo de Chavela Space Observatory.

Incredible place full of antennas and radio telescopes to listen to space. The antennas impress, for their size and for the work they do. Check their official website to see if the museum is open or not.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the hike to Cerro de La Almenara?
faqs.answer: You can find parking near the Madrid Deep Space Communications Complex (MDSCC), which serves as the starting point for this circular route. This allows for convenient access to the trailhead.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for dogs?
faqs.answer: Yes, this trail is generally dog-friendly. However, always keep your dog on a leash, especially in areas with livestock or wildlife, and ensure you clean up after them to preserve the natural environment.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The trail is enjoyable year-round, but spring and autumn are ideal. Autumn offers particularly beautiful colors, while spring brings pleasant temperatures. If hiking in summer, be sure to carry plenty of water as natural sources can be dry.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for this hike?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to hike this trail. It's freely accessible to the public.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations for hiking in the Sierra de Guadarrama area?
faqs.answer: While there are no specific permits for this trail, when hiking in the Sierra de Guadarrama region, it's always good practice to follow 'leave no trace' principles: stay on marked paths, do not disturb wildlife, and take all your trash with you. Respect local flora and fauna.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Cerro de La Almenara trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied, typical of the Sierra Oeste of Madrid. You'll encounter well-maintained dehesas with oak trees in lower sections and scrubland as you gain elevation. The path mostly follows tracks and accessible trails, but the final ascent to the summit involves steeper sections and can include a small scramble over loose stones, requiring careful footing.

faqs.question: What notable sights can I expect to see along the route?
faqs.answer: This route offers several unique sights. You'll pass by the impressive antennas of the Madrid Deep Space Communications Complex. Other highlights include the historic Navahonda Hermitage, the distinctive Almenara Pine Group, and of course, the summit of La Almenara itself, offering panoramic views.

faqs.question: What kind of views can I expect from the summit of La Almenara?
faqs.answer: From the summit of La Almenara, you'll be treated to expansive panoramic views. These include the surrounding dehesas, nearby smaller mountain ranges, El Escorial, and on clear days, you might even spot distant sights of Madrid and Toledo. It's a truly rewarding vista.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other trails. You'll find it intersects with parts of the 'De Valencia a Lisboa (Etapa desde San Lorenzo del Escorial a Pelayos de la Presa)' trail, as well as several segments of the 'Robledo de Chavela a Navahonda' path.

faqs.question: What is the historical significance of Cerro de La Almenara?
faqs.answer: Cerro de La Almenara, meaning 'place of light' in Arabic, has a rich history. It served as a watchtower during the Muslim invasion of Spain, where bonfires were lit to warn Toledo of approaching Christian troops. Archaeological remains from this period can still be found at the summit, adding a historical layer to your hike.

faqs.question: Is this a challenging hike, and what should I be aware of?
faqs.answer: This is considered a moderate hike, requiring good physical condition. While much of it is accessible, the final ascent to the peak can be more challenging due to loose stones and a small scramble. Some describe this final 'trepada' as hard, so sure-footedness is essential. Be prepared for varied terrain and carry enough water.
